content: I sometimes feel like I'm spamming when I'm one of the only people on here in the morning in the UK, responding to discussions that I've missed by being asleep. Is there anything I can do about this? Maybe I should just shift my time spent on the platform later in the day...
i semi-relate insofar as i keep a horrible sleep schedule and end up being on here at hours when it's very quiet

Definitely don't worry about it; one of the benefits of Zulip is the ability to do that in a reasonable way, without creating a huge wall of text that's hard to link back to past discussions, as you might have done on IRC. You should really think of each topic as mostly separate in this sense; and I doubt you would call it spamming to wake up in the morning and send one or a few messages each to many different and unrelated IRC channels.

I think that's orthogonal to sending a lot of text to a single topic; but I also don't think that's necessarily bad at all, if you've got a lot to say. It's the content that decides spam-ness, not the quantity of text. And if it does become too much, or goes off topic, the messages can be moved to a different topic!
Haha, too perfect; I only just realized this topic was from almost four weeks ago. :big_smile: But that's okay, too!

Actually it's a good thing you brought this back up, since one of the things that motivated that (and which is more relevant now) is the fear that even in this safe, friendly, online space, white male overconfident voices like my own could be dominating, to the detriment of less frequent users. I'm glad I wasn't over contributing, but despite the reassurances I am sure it could happen. Be considerate out there, folks :heart:

This doesn't prove anything, but what you said reminded me of the recently added "Zulip for researchers" page — where this community makes an appearance, by the way! (Search for "Category Theory")

Particularly these points:

Zulip’s topic-based threading model solves these problems:

  • Participants in any time zone can send messages and expect to get a reply and have an effective (potentially asynchronous) conversation with the rest of the community.
  • ...
  • Topics make it easier to provide a safe, welcoming, online community. Asking a question never has to feel like an interruption of an ongoing conversation or like one's sticking one's neck out.
("Zulip for open source" is good as well.)

https://twitter.com/_julesh_/status/1271011191129866247 :tada:

The category theory Zulip gets top billing on Zulip's "for research" page https://zulipchat.com/for/research/ (*) Preemptive invite link: https://categorytheory.zulipchat.com/join/fkwcui4dovyqduiw24dots2p/

- julesh (@_julesh_)
How do you think we got to 1000 members? First time I shared the link it got 20k eyeballs and 480 link clicks, and I also shared it a bunch more times since then
I've been advertising each ACT@UCR talk to my hordes of Twitter followers, and telling them they can discuss the talks here.
So yeah, we've got lots of members, some of whom actually do stuff.
